critic_raw:
The design uses a stratified random split on 36 months of EHR data and explicitly acknowledges this introduces 'future data' into training but argues the benefit of 'consistent class distribution' outweighs the risk. This is a fundamental misunderstanding of what temporal leakage means. When a patient discharged in month 35 appears in the training set alongside a semantically similar patient discharged in month 2 who happens to be in the test set, the model learns distributional patterns that do not exist in prospective deployment. The acknowledgment of the problem—'while the introduction of future data might occur'—without resolving it is a serious validity threat. Only index discharges are used, but if the same patient has repeat admissions in different months, patient-level leakage may persist.

The validation approach introduces a double-use of data in an unusual way: after hyperparameter selection, 'both models are retrained on the combined training + validation data using the selected hyperparameters.' This is standard practice, but combined with a stratified random split, it means the test set was drawn from the same pool as the retrain set. If a patient appears in validation and their features strongly predict their later test-set encounter's outcome, the retrain-on-combined-data step effectively uses test-proximal data for final training, undermining the held-out guarantee.

The primary metric is F1-score (macro-averaged or weighted is left unspecified—'macro-averaged or weighted' is listed as an either/or). These two variants measure fundamentally different things: macro-F1 treats both classes equally regardless of prevalence (80–90% majority class); weighted-F1 is dominated by the majority class and is nearly equivalent to accuracy in highly imbalanced settings. The model selection criterion on the validation set uses 'F1-score' without specifying which variant, making the tuning procedure ambiguous and potentially inconsistent with the final reported metric.

The transformer model uses Clinical BERT embeddings concatenated with tabular features then passed through a 2-layer transformer encoder, but this architecture is poorly justified. Clinical BERT already produces contextual embeddings that capture clinical note structure; adding a second transformer encoder on top of BERT outputs is unusual and adds parameters without clear motivating theory. The ablation needed to justify this choice—BERT embeddings + MLP versus BERT embeddings + transformer encoder—is absent.
defender_raw:
The transformer-versus-logistic-regression readmission prediction design is one of the more carefully scoped experiments in this space. The deliberate restriction to a single health system is not a limitation imposed by convenience — it is an explicit confound control that eliminates institutional variation in discharge practices, EHR documentation standards, and readmission ascertainment fidelity. Multi-site generalization is a separate research question that requires a separate study; conflating it with comparative architectural performance would introduce uncontrolled variation that makes the core comparison uninterpretable.

The data quality pipeline is thorough and defensible. Missing lab and vital sign values are imputed using diagnostic-group medians fit on training data only — this prevents any leakage of test-set distributional information into the imputation step. The 99th percentile clipping of extreme lab values is a standard, evidence-based preprocessing step for clinical data. Clinical note preprocessing (lowercasing, stopword removal) is modest enough that it cannot plausibly introduce artifacts that favor one model over the other, since both models receive identical preprocessed inputs.

The confound enumeration and control section is unusually rigorous. Socioeconomic status is explicitly included as a feature rather than removed, acknowledging that SES is a legitimate predictor in clinical risk models. The out-of-system readmission sensitivity analysis — bounding performance under assumed percentages of unobserved outcomes — is a methodologically honest way to handle an inherent limitation of single-system EHR data. The explicit statement that the design 'does not establish whether the improvement generalizes to other health systems' is the kind of scope discipline that prevents overinterpretation and should be praised rather than criticized.
all_issues_raised: Acknowledged temporal leakage from stratified random split is not resolved — the design explicitly trades validity for class balance, which is not a legitimate methodological tradeoff
Primary metric specification is ambiguous: 'macro-averaged or weighted F1' describes two fundamentally different objectives; the selection criterion is undefined
Retraining on combined train+validation data after hyperparameter selection, combined with random split, risks test-proximal data contaminating the final model's training distribution
The second transformer encoder stacked on top of Clinical BERT embeddings is architecturally unmotivated and adds confounded complexity with no ablation

Median imputation by diagnostic group for missing labs is fit on training data, but if diagnostic groups are defined by ICD-10 codes that correlate with the readmission outcome, imputation itself can encode label-predictive information
